---
title: "TD_P004: No Parallel Processing in Shiny Cloud (CLOUD_P001)"
subtitle: "Prohibition of furrr/future parallel processing in Posit Connect Cloud and shinyapps.io"
chapter: "CH05"
category: "principle"
number: "TD_P004"
alias: "CLOUD_P001"
date-created: "2025-01-08"
date-modified: "2025-01-08"
author: "Claude"
type: "principle"
law: "Testing & Deployment Law"
article-number: "Article 4"
derives_from:
  - "CH00.MP099: Realtime Progress Reporting"
  - "TD_P001: Deployment Patterns"
influences:
  - "Future parallel processing implementations"
  - "OpenAI API integration patterns"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Article Overview {#overview}

**Testing & Deployment Law Article 4 (No Parallel Processing in Shiny Cloud)**

Also known as **CLOUD_P001**, this principle establishes a prohibition on using `furrr::future_map()` or `future::multisession` parallel processing in Shiny applications deployed to Posit Connect Cloud or shinyapps.io.

:::{.callout-important}
## Core Principle
**NEVER use `furrr` parallel processing in Shiny Cloud environments.** Functions defined within Shiny reactive contexts capture their entire parent environment (closure), which can exceed the `future.globals.maxSize` limit and cause deployment failures.
:::

# Section 1: Problem Statement {#problem}

## Subsection 1: The Closure Capture Problem {#closure-capture}

When a function is defined inside a Shiny reactive context, R captures the entire parent environment as a closure. This includes:

- All reactive values
- Data frames in memory
- Session objects
- Global environment references

### Example of the Problem

```r
# ❌ This will FAIL in Shiny Cloud
observeEvent(input$run_scoring, {

  # This function captures the ENTIRE parent environment
  score_review <- function(review, attributes) {
    # ... scoring logic
  }

  # furrr attempts to serialize score_review to send to workers
  # But the closure includes 800+ MB of parent environment!
  results <- furrr::future_map(reviews, score_review)
})
```

## Subsection 2: Error Message {#error-message}

The characteristic error message:

```
The total size of the 13 globals exported for future expression is 817.59 MiB.
This exceeds the maximum allowed size 500.00 MiB (future.globals.maxSize)

The three largest globals are:
- 'score_review' (817.51 MiB)  ← The function closure!
- 'chat_api' (26.68 KiB)
- 'review_texts' (14.65 KiB)
```

## Subsection 3: Why This Happens {#why-happens}

1. **Shiny's reactive model**: Functions defined in reactive contexts inherit their environment
2. **future's serialization**: `future::multisession` must serialize all globals to send to workers
3. **Size limitation**: `future.globals.maxSize` defaults to 500MB
4. **Cloud constraints**: Posit Connect Cloud has limited memory and stricter resource controls

# Section 2: Prohibited Patterns {#prohibited}

## Subsection 1: Direct Prohibition {#direct-prohibition}

The following patterns are **PROHIBITED** in Shiny Cloud deployments:

```r
# ❌ PROHIBITED: furrr parallel processing
library(future)
library(furrr)

future::plan(future::multisession, workers = 4)
results <- furrr::future_map(data, process_fn)
future::plan(future::sequential)
```

## Subsection 2: Also Prohibited {#also-prohibited}

```r
# ❌ PROHIBITED: future with multisession
future::plan(future::multisession)
results <- future::future_lapply(data, process_fn)

# ❌ PROHIBITED: parallel package in Shiny
library(parallel)
cl <- makeCluster(4)
results <- parLapply(cl, data, process_fn)
stopCluster(cl)
```

# Section 3: Approved Alternatives {#alternatives}

## Subsection 1: Sequential Processing with Progress {#sequential-progress}

**APPROVED PATTERN**: Use sequential `for` loops with `setProgress()` for user feedback.

```r
# ✅ APPROVED: Sequential processing with progress
results <- withProgress(message = "Processing...", value = 0.1, {
  results_list <- list()
  total <- length(data)

  for (i in seq_len(total)) {
    setProgress(
      value = 0.1 + 0.8 * (i / total),
      detail = sprintf("Item %d/%d", i, total)
    )

    results_list[[i]] <- tryCatch({
      process_fn(data[[i]])
    }, error = function(e) {
      message(sprintf("Error at %d: %s", i, e$message))
      NA
    })
  }

  setProgress(value = 0.9, detail = "Finalizing...")
  results_list
})
```

## Subsection 2: Batch API Calls {#batch-api}

**APPROVED PATTERN**: Reduce API calls by batching requests.

```r
# ❌ BEFORE: 750 API calls (50 reviews × 15 attributes)
for (review in reviews) {
  for (attr in attributes) {
    score <- call_api(review, attr)  # 15 calls per review
  }
}

# ✅ AFTER: 50 API calls (1 per review, all attributes at once)
for (review in reviews) {
  scores <- call_api_batch(review, all_attributes)  # 1 call per review
}
```

## Subsection 3: Background Queue System {#queue-system}

For very long operations, consider implementing a queue system:

```r
# ✅ APPROVED: Queue-based processing
# 1. User submits job → stored in database
# 2. Background worker processes jobs
# 3. User notified when complete

# This is out of scope for this principle but mentioned as an alternative
```

# Section 4: Implementation Examples {#examples}

## Subsection 1: BrandEdge Scoring (Before/After) {#brandedge-example}

### Before (Broken)
```r
# ❌ This caused crashes
n_workers <- min(4, parallel::detectCores() - 1)
future::plan(future::multisession, workers = n_workers)

scores_list <- furrr::future_map(
  seq_len(total_reviews),
  function(i) {
    score_review(review_texts[i], attributes)
  },
  .options = furrr::furrr_options(seed = TRUE)
)

future::plan(future::sequential)
```

### After (Fixed)
```r
# ✅ Sequential processing with progress
all_scores <- withProgress(message = "Scoring...", value = 0.1, {
  scores_list <- list()
  for (i in seq_len(total_reviews)) {
    setProgress(
      value = 0.1 + 0.8 * (i / total_reviews),
      detail = sprintf("%d/%d (%s)", i, total_reviews, review_brands[i])
    )
    scores_list[[i]] <- tryCatch({
      score_review(review_texts[i], attributes, review_idx = i)
    }, error = function(e) {
      rep(NA_real_, length(attributes))
    })
  }
  scores_list
})
```

## Subsection 2: InsightForge Scoring (Efficiency Fix) {#insightforge-example}

### Before (Inefficient + Broken)
```r
# ❌ 750 API calls + parallel crash
# For each review, call API 15 times (once per attribute)
for (review in reviews) {
  prompts <- lapply(attributes, function(attr) {
    prepare_prompt(var_id = "score_attribute", attr = attr)  # singular
  })
  scores <- lapply(prompts, function(p) chat_api(p))  # 15 calls!
}
```

### After (Efficient + Fixed)
```r
# ✅ 50 API calls, sequential, stable
for (i in seq_len(total_reviews)) {
  setProgress(value = i / total_reviews)

  # One API call per review, scores ALL attributes at once
  prompt <- prepare_prompt(
    var_id = "score_attributes",  # PLURAL - batch scoring
    attributes = paste(attrs, collapse = ", "),
    review_text = review_texts[i]
  )

  response <- chat_api(prompt)
  scores <- parse_json_scores(response)
}
```

# Section 5: Scope and Exceptions {#scope}

## Subsection 1: Applies To {#applies-to}

This principle applies to:

- **Posit Connect Cloud** (connect.posit.cloud)
- **shinyapps.io** (all plans)
- Any resource-constrained Shiny hosting environment

## Subsection 2: Exceptions {#exceptions}

This principle does NOT apply to:

- **Local development**: Parallel processing works fine locally
- **Self-hosted Posit Connect Server**: Can configure `future.globals.maxSize` and allocate more resources
- **Non-Shiny R scripts**: Scripts without reactive context don't have closure capture issues

# Section 6: Performance Comparison {#performance}

## Subsection 1: BrandEdge Results {#brandedge-results}

| Metric | Before (Parallel) | After (Sequential) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Stability | ❌ Crashes in Cloud | ✅ Stable |
| API Calls (50 reviews) | 50 | 50 |
| Speed | ~2-3 min (when working) | ~3-5 min |
| User Experience | ❌ Frozen screen | ✅ Real-time progress |

## Subsection 2: InsightForge Results {#insightforge-results}

| Metric | Before (Per-attr × Parallel) | After (Batch × Sequential) |
|--------|------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Stability | ❌ Crashes | ✅ Stable |
| API Calls (50 reviews × 15 attrs) | 750 | **50** |
| Speed | ~15-30 min (theoretical) | **~3-5 min** |
| Efficiency Gain | - | **15x fewer API calls** |

# Implementation Checklist {#checklist}

:::{.callout-note}
## CLOUD_P001 Compliance Checklist

When deploying Shiny apps to Cloud environments:

- [ ] No `library(furrr)` in production code
- [ ] No `library(future)` with `multisession` plan
- [ ] No `parallel::makeCluster()` usage
- [ ] All long operations use `withProgress()` + `setProgress()`
- [ ] API calls are batched where possible
- [ ] Sequential `for` loops are used instead of parallel map
- [ ] Error handling with `tryCatch()` in loops
- [ ] Progress messages are user-friendly
:::

# Related Principles {#related}

- **MP099**: Realtime Progress Reporting - Use progress indicators for user feedback
- **TD_P001**: Deployment Patterns - General deployment guidelines
- **Issue #141**: Parallel Scoring Implementation - Origin of this principle

# Conclusion {#conclusion}

The `furrr` parallel processing approach that works locally will fail in Shiny Cloud environments due to closure serialization limits. Always use sequential processing with real-time progress updates for Cloud deployments. The performance penalty is minimal when combined with batch API optimizations, and the stability gain is essential for production use.

---

*Based on: Testing & Deployment Law Article 4*
*Origin: Issue #141 - Parallel Scoring Implementation failure in Posit Connect Cloud*
*Date: 2025-01-08*
